column1:
Which of the following correctly describes an IVC aneurysm?

A: Rarely leads to thrombus formation
B: Commonly associated with blue toe syndrome
C: Most IVC aneurysms are saccular
D: Must be associated with interrupted IVC to be considered a true IVC aneurysm
column2:
Answer: C. Most IVC aneurysms are saccular

Feedback:
IVC aneurysms can be saccular or fusiform, but the saccular formation is slightly more common. Aortic aneurysms are more
commonly fusiform.

column1:
The images displayed are from an aortic Doppler evaluation on a ER trauma patient. What should you evaluate next?

A: iliac arteries for dissection
B: iliac veins for thrombus
C: renal arteries for stenosis
D: IVC for thrombus
column2:
Answer: A. iliac arteries for dissection

Feedback:
There is a dissection present in the aorta. You should evaluate the proximal and distal end of the dissection to assess the
extent.

column1:
Where is the Doppler cursor placed in the aorta to obtain the velocity used in the renal aortic ratio?

A: just below the diaphragm
B: at the level of the renal artery origins
C: 2 cm above the aortic bifurcation
D: at the location of the highest recorded velocity
column2:
Answer: B. at the level of the renal artery origins

Feedback:
The aortic velocity should be obtained at the level of the renal artery origins when evaluating renal arterial flow.

column1:
Which of the following procedures can be performed on a patient in renal failure?

A: CT scan with contrast
B: Angiography
C: Duplex ultrasound
D: MRA exam with contrast
column2:
Answer: C. Duplex ultrasound

Feedback:
Renal disease or failure is a contraindication for the use of radiology imaging contrast.

column1:
Which of the following vessels are a common source for a Type II endoleak?

A: common iliac artery and gonadal arteries
B: inferior mesenteric artery and lumbar arteries
C: celiac axis and superior mesenteric artery
D: left gastric artery and right hepatic artery
column2:
Answer: B. inferior mesenteric artery and lumbar arteries

Feedback:
Type II endoleak : leak at the branch vessels; will see retrograde flow into the aneurysm sac and increase in AAA diameter;
inferior mesenteric artery, internal iliac, lumbar arteries and accessory renal arteries are potential sources for the leak

column1:
When evaluating a liver transplant, you must use angle correction during Doppler evaluation of:

A: portal veins only
B: hepatic arteries and veins
C: hepatic arteries and portal veins
D: hepatic arteries only
column2:
Answer: C. hepatic arteries and portal veins

Feedback:
When evaluating the velocity in a vessel using PW Doppler, angle correction must be used to obtain an accurate velocity. All
arterial evaluations with Doppler require angle correction. While most veins do not require angle correction because velocity is
not evaluated, the portal vein velocity is measured to assess a liver transplant and angle correction is necessary.

column1:
Nutcracker syndrome is defined as:

A: abnormal compression of the celiac axis with respiration
B: entrapment of the subclavian artery by structures in the shoulder/neck
C: abnormal compression of the left renal vein by the SMA and aorta
D: entrapment of the popliteal artery seen most commonly in runners
column2:
Answer: C. abnormal compression of the left renal vein by the SMA and aorta

Feedback:
Nutcracker syndrome is defined as abnormal compression of the left renal vein by the SMA and aorta. It can lead to renal vein
congestion and thrombosis.

column1:
The Doppler evaluation displays a waveform from the hepatic artery and the portal vein. Which of the following statements is correct?

A: The tracing is abnormal because vessels should both display hepatopetal flow
B: The tracing is normal because the flow is in opposing directions
C: The tracing is abnormal because baseline is inverted incorrectly
D: The tracing is abnormal because vessels should both display hepatofugal flow
column2:
Answer: A. The tracing is abnormal because vessels should both display hepatopetal flow

Feedback:
The hepatic artery and the portal vein both carry blood into the liver (hepatopetal). The attached image demonstrates flow
reversal in the portal vein which is a common sign of portal HTN.

column1:
A patient with RUQ abdomen pain and bloating is referred to rule out Budd Chiari syndrome. What vessels will be evaluated for this diagnosis?

A: Aorta, SMA and celiac axis
B: Renal arteries
C: Hepatic veins
D: Renal veins
column2:
Answer: C. Hepatic veins

Feedback:
Budd Chiari syndrome refers to chronic obstruction of the hepatic veins. It can be related to thrombus formation and extrinsic
compression of the vessels.

column1:
Which of the following can lead to systolic flow reversal in the hepatic veins?

A: portal HTN
B: IVC thrombus at the level of the renal veins
C: Budd Chiari Syndrome
D: significant tricuspid regurgitation
column2:
Answer: D. significant tricuspid regurgitation

Feedback:
Tricuspid regurgitation is blood flow that falls back into the right atrium. It can cause the right atrium to dilate and the chamber
pressure to increase. If the pressure in the RA increases, inflow from the IVC (and hepatic veins) will be disrupted and flow
reversal in systole can occur.

column1:
Aneurysm formation affects the ____________________________, while atherosclerotic disease affects the _______________________.

A: media and adventitia layers of the vessel wall, intimal layer of the vessel wall
B: proximal arteries, distal arteries
C: intimal layer of the vessel wall, media and adventitia layers of the vessel wall
D: extracranial arteries, intracranial arteries
column2:
Answer: A. media and adventitia layers of the vessel wall, intimal layer of the vessel wall

Feedback:
Aneurysm formation is caused by a breakdown of the media and adventitia layers of the vessel wall. Atherosclerotic disease
causes formation of plaque within the intimal layer of the vessel wall. Both disorders can be found in any artery in the body.

column1:
A patient presents with splenomegaly and multiple, small, tortuous vessels are visualized in the porta hepatis area of the liver. These vessels most likely represent:

A: Duplicated hepatic artery
B: Cavernous transformation
C: Dilated extrahepatic biliary system
D: Portal aneurysm
column2:
Answer: B. Cavernous transformation

Feedback:
Cavernous transformation of the vessels at the porta hepatis refers to the formation of varices from the increased portal
pressure with portal HTN. Accessory veins dilate at the porta hepatis due to the backlog of blood flow. These vessels are small
and tortuous. Splenomegaly is another sign of portal HTN and a backlog in the blood flow in the portal system.

column1:
A 55yr old with a history of alcoholism, jaundice and cirrhosis is referred for a liver Doppler exam. Which of the following is an expected abnormal finding?

A: respiratory phasicity in portal vein flow
B: multiphasic flow in the hepatic veins
C: dilated IVC
D: increased resistance in the hepatic artery
column2:
Answer: D. increased resistance in the hepatic artery

Feedback:
When an organ has reduced function, it resists the normal amount of blood flow needed. Resistance in the hepatic artery will
increase with the increase in resistance to flow in the cirrhotic liver tissue. The coronary vein (AKA left gastric vein) and the
umbilical vein will dilate with significant portal HTN. The IVC is not usually affected by isolated cirrhosis. The hepatic vein flow
will be multiphasic in the normal patient. Normal portal venous flow demonstrates mild respiratory phasicity.

column1:
A tardus parvus waveform is the result of:

A: significant proximal arterial stenosis
B: partial venous obstruction
C: distal arterial obstruction
D: <50% proximal arterial stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. significant proximal arterial stenosis

Feedback:
A tardus parvus waveform is associated with significant stenosis that severely decreases flow through an artery. This waveform
is described as low velocity and resistance with a late systolic peak.
EXAMPLE OF TARDUS PARVUS WAVEFORM IN RENAL STENOSIS

column1:
A patient is referred for an ultrasound of the aorta due to a history of Ehler-Danlos syndrome. What are you looking for on the exam?

A: Significant soft plaque formation in the distal aorta and proximal iliac arteries
B: Significant fibrous tissue formation surrounding the distal aorta that is compressing the vessel
C: Aneurysm
D: Absence of all anterior branches
column2:
Answer: C. Aneurysm

Feedback:
Ehler-Danlos syndrome is a genetic disorder that negatively affects collagen formation. The reduction in collagen will result in
less rigid arteries and increased risk of aneurysm formation.

column1:
If a patient complains of a recent onset of uncontrollable systemic HTN with average BP readings exceeding 170/90mmHg, what vascular cause should be suspected?

A: renal artery stenosis
B: subclavian stenosis
C: portal HTN
D: hepatic congestion
column2:
Answer: A. renal artery stenosis

Feedback:
A sudden increase in systemic BP with no response to medication is usually associated with renovascular HTN caused by renal
artery stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following calculations is necessary to accurately diagnose renovascular HTN?

A: systolic / diastolic ratio
B: peak pressure gradient
C: parenchymal index
D: renal-aortic ratio
column2:
Answer: D. renal-aortic ratio

Feedback:
The renal-aortic ratio compares the velocities in the renal artery with the velocity of the aorta in the mid-segment. >3.5 indicates
renal artery stenosis.

column1:
While performing an aortic duplex exam, you identify an intimal flap that begins just below the renal arteries and measures 2.5cm in length. According to the Stanford Classification, this is a _______________________ and the DeBakey Classification calls it a ___________________.

A: Type A dissection, Type III dissection
B: Type A dissection, Type I dissection
C: Juxtarenal dissection, Type II dissection
D: Type B dissection, Type III dissection
column2:
Answer: D. Type B dissection, Type III dissection

Feedback:
Stanford Classification:
Type A: dissections that involve the ascending aorta and arch (proximal to the origin of the left subclavian artery)
Type B: dissections that involve the aorta from the left subclavian artery to the iliac bifurcation
DeBakey Classification:
Type I: dissections that involve the ascending and descending aorta (= Stanford A)
Type II: dissections that involve only the ascending aorta (= Stanford A)
Type III: dissections that involve the descending aorta only, starting after the origin of the left subclavian artery (= Stanford B)

column1:
Which renal arteries are evaluated with Doppler in an ultrasound exam that is used to perform a direct evaluation for renal artery stenosis?

A: main renal artery
B: aorta
C: segmental and arcuate arteries
D: main renal artery, segmental and arcuate arteries
column2:
Answer: A. main renal artery

Feedback:
A direct evaluation uses Doppler to evaluate the velocity in the main renal artery segments. The indirect evaluation uses
Doppler to evaluate the flow in the segmental/parenchymal arteries. The RI and AT values are assessed and the shape of the
waveform is considered in the diagnosis. (not peak velocity) The indirect evaluation looks for signs of stenosis in the main renal
artery by looking at the flow in the organ distally.

column1:
The images are from a Doppler evaluation of the kidneys. What do you expect to find when you evaluate the main renal arteries?

A: both renal arteries will demonstrate stenosis
B: both vessels will demonstrate normal velocities
C: left renal artery will demonstrate flow velocities >200cm/s and the right renal artery will demonstrate normal velocities
D: right renal artery will demonstrate flow velocities >200cm/s and the left renal artery will demonstrate normal velocities
column2:
Answer: C. left renal artery will demonstrate flow velocities >200cm/s and the right renal artery will demonstrate normal velocities

Feedback:
The left intrarenal Doppler tracing demonstrates a tardus parvus waveform indicative of proximal renal stenosis. The right
intrarenal artery demonstrates normal acceleration and deceleration.
Image adapted from P. Karmin, MD. https://www.imagingrealm.com/vascular.html

column1:
Which of the following is an indirect sign of an unstable AAA sac with an endoleak?

A: Echolucent areas within the sac
B: Reduction in aneurysm shape and/or sac size
C: Absence of pulsatility of the sac
D: Triphasic flow in the iliac arteries
column2:
Answer: A. Echolucent areas within the sac

Feedback:
Potential signs of an unstable AAA sac:
Echolucent areas within the sac - fresh blood leaking out of graft and into sac
Changes in aneurysm shape and/or sac size - increase in size and shape changes indicate blood flowing into the sac
Increased pulsatility of the sac - the walls of the AAA become thinner as it stretches with fresh blood internally, this causes the
sac to pulse more with cardiac contractions.

column1:
Which of the following is most suggestive of renal transplant rejection?

A: oliguria
B: hyperbilirubinemia
C: urinary frequency and urgency
D: reduced serum levels of BUN and creatinine
column2:
Answer: A. oliguria

Feedback:
Oliguria or anuria are important clinical indications for renal transplant failure. If the kidney is not producing urine, it is failing.
Other signs include elevated BUN and creatinine levels, hematuria and new onset of HTN.

column1:
How do the microbubbles in an ultrasound contrast agent change the image?

A: increase the reflectivity of flowing blood
B: increase the resistance to flowing blood to make it more visible
C: increase vasodilation to move the bubbles more quickly so they are visible to ultrasound
D: microbubbles cannot be used to evaluate arterial flow due to embolism risk
column2:
Answer: A. increase the reflectivity of flowing blood

Feedback:
Microbubbles in an ultrasound contrast agent increase the reflectivity of flowing blood.

column1:
Which of the following is not a characteristic of a high-grade stenosis?

A: Laminar flow just distal to the site of stenosis
B: Spectral broadening just distal to the site of stenosis
C: Loss of pulsatility of waveform distally
D: Extremely high velocity signals at the site of stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. Laminar flow just distal to the site of stenosis

Feedback:

column1:
The most common reason for underestimation of arterial stenosis is:

A: insonation of a vein and artery simultaneously
B: improper sample volume angle
C: improper sample volume location
D: insonation of a vein instead of an artery
column2:
Answer: C. improper sample volume location

Feedback:
The most common reason for underestimation of arterial stenosis is improper sample volume location. Anytime stenosis is
suspected, the cursor should be walked through the area of stenosis to locate the area of highest velocity. Lack of proper
sample placement can cause you to miss the area of highest velocity and therefore underestimate the stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following describes the proper way to measure an abdominal aortic aneurysm?

A: outer wall to inner wall
B: outer wall to outer wall
C: inner wall to inner wall
D: inner wall to outer wall
column2:
Answer: B. outer wall to outer wall

Feedback:
The aorta should be measured from outer wall to outer wall. The AP dimension is considered the most reliable dimension for
assessing a possible AAA.

column1:
The image demonstrates a _____________________ aneurysm.

A: Fusiform
B: Berry
C: Saccular
D: Dissecting
column2:
Answer: A. Fusiform

Feedback:
Note the uniform dilatation on the anterior and posterior aspects of the vessel consistent with a fusiform AAA.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes an aortic aneurysm?

A: Patients with a popliteal artery aneurysm rarely have an associated AAA
B: The AP diameter is considered the most reliable dimension for diagnosis of a AAA
C: AAA with a diameter of 4.5cm or greater will require surgical intervention
D: The most common complication of a AAA is thrombus formation in the IVC
column2:
Answer: B. The AP diameter is considered the most reliable dimension for diagnosis of a AAA

Feedback:
The AP diameter is considered the most reliable dimension for diagnosis of a AAA. AAA with a diameter of 5.5cm or greater will
require surgical intervention. The most common complication of a AAA is rupture. >60% of patients that have a popliteal artery
aneurysm also have a AAA.

column1:
Which of the following is NOT a potential acute complication of a liver transplant?

A: portal vein stenosis
B: portal thrombosis
C: cirrhosis
D: hepatic artery thrombosis
column2:
Answer: C. cirrhosis

Feedback:
Cirrhosis is a chronic liver disease. Thrombosis and extrinsic vessel narrowing/compression are acute complications that can
occur in a liver transplant.

column1:
A patient presents with a script that requests an aortic Doppler evaluation due to Leriche syndrome. What are you looking for on the exam?

A: extensive diffuse atherosclerotic disease causing obstruction of the distal aorta
B: the formation of multiple aneurysms of the aortic segments
C: monophasic flow in all segments of the aorta due to coarctation of the aorta in the chest
D: agenesis of the anterior branches of the aorta
column2:
Answer: A. extensive diffuse atherosclerotic disease causing obstruction of the distal aorta

Feedback:
Leriche syndrome refers to extensive diffuse atherosclerotic disease causing obstruction of the distal aorta and bilateral lower
extremity symptoms.

column1:
Renal artery stenosis is considered hemodynamically significant when it is greater than ______.

A: 50%
B: 60%
C: 70%
D: 80%
column2:
Answer: B. 60%

Feedback:
Renal artery stenosis is considered hemodynamically significant when it is greater than 60%.

column1:
Hemodynamically significant stenosis of the celiac axis is diagnosed with the PSV greater than?

A: 1.8m/s
B: 2.0m/s
C: 2.5m/s
D: 2.75m/s
column2:
Answer: B. 2.0m/s

Feedback:
Hemodynamically significant stenosis of the celiac axis is diagnosed with the PSV greater than 2.0 m/s.

column1:
What is caput medusae?

A: lower extremity varicosities on the anterior thigh
B: tortuous vessels around the umbilicus caused by portal HTN
C: formation of gastroesophageal varices caused by portal HTN
D: lower extremity varicosities on the anterior knee
column2:
Answer: B. tortuous vessels around the umbilicus caused by portal HTN

Feedback:
Caput medusae refers to dilated, tortuous vessels around the umbilicus caused by portal HTN. The vessels are visible just
beneath the subcutaneous tissue, similar to varicosities in the legs

column1:
If a patient has a history of _______________, they have an increased risk for carotid dissection.

A: Marfan syndrome
B: Raynaud syndrome
C: Thoracic outlet syndrome
D: Budd Chiari
column2:
Answer: A. Marfan syndrome

Feedback:
Marfan syndrome is a connective tissue disorder that affects the heart and blood vessels. It is often associated with dilatation
and/or aneurysm of the aorta, which can lead to intimal dissection.

column1:
The images below are from a renal artery duplex examination. Review the information on the images and choose the waveform below that is most consistent with the expected waveform for the right renal parenchymal arteries. Image A Image B Image C Image D

A: Image A
B: Image B
C: Image C
D: Image D
column2:
Answer: B. Image B

Feedback:
The right renal artery origin demonstrates significant stenosis. The renal parenchymal arteries will demonstrate low velocity
flow with slow acceleration and deceleration (tardus parvus).

column1:
The type of plaque that carries the greatest risk of embolization is ______________.

A: soft
B: complex
C: calcific
D: soft and complex
column2:
Answer: A. soft

Feedback:
Soft plaque has the greatest risk of embolization due to its composition and decreased adhesive levels. Complex and calcific
plaque have become increasingly fibrous and more easily adheres to the vessel wall.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding the Doppler tracing from the renal artery?

A: The renal to aortic ratio is most likely below 3.5
B: Normal flow pattern and velocity are demonstrated
C: According to RAS diagnostic criteria, Stenosis > 60% is demonstrated
D: Normal flow pattern and velocity are demonstrated but an incorrect Doppler angle is used to assess the flow causing aliasing to occur
column2:
Answer: C. According to RAS diagnostic criteria, Stenosis > 60% is demonstrated

Feedback:
Renal artery stenosis > 60% is indicated when the PSV is greater than 2.0m/s and the renal aortic ratio is over 3.5. A 60%
stenosis is considered significant and treatment is required. There are no other diagnostic criteria used to stage RAS at 75% and
85% stenosis etc.

column1:
An abnormal renal-aortic ratio would be a ratio ___________.

A: <3.5
B: >1.8
C: <1.8
D: >3.5
column2:
Answer: D. >3.5

Feedback:
The renal aortic ratio is calculated by dividing the peak renal artery velocity by the peak velocity in the aorta. A value <3.5
indicates a normal exam. This ratio means that the blood in the renal arteries is moving 3.5 times faster than the blood in the
aorta. Higher ratios mean higher renal artery velocities = stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following describes the most common flow pattern seen in a Type II endoleak?

A: bidirectional
B: continuous, phasic flow
C: monophasic, low resistance
D: monophasic retrograde flow
column2:
Answer: A. bidirectional

Feedback:
Flow within a Type I leak resembles the flow within the graft. Flow in a Type II leak usually demonstrates a bidirectional flow
pattern because the Doppler waveform morphology reflects the end source resistance. A single branch vessel entering the sac
will demonstrate bidirectional flow.

column1:
Which renal arteries are evaluated with Doppler in an ultrasound exam that is used to perform an indirect evaluation for renal artery stenosis?

A: main renal artery
B: segmental and parenchymal arteries
C: main renal artery, segmental and parenchymal arteries
D: aorta
column2:
Answer: B. segmental and parenchymal arteries

Feedback:
A direct evaluation uses Doppler to evaluate the velocity in the main renal artery segments. The indirect evaluation uses
Doppler to evaluate the flow in the segmental/parenchymal arteries. The RI and AT values are assessed and the shape of the
waveform is considered in the diagnosis. (not peak velocity) The indirect evaluation looks for signs of stenosis in the main renal
artery by looking at the flow in the organ distally. The aorta is not a "renal artery".

column1:
Which of the following is true regarding scrotal varicoceles?

A: usually occurs on left side
B: strong association with history of cryptorchidism
C: veins will reduce in size with the Valsalva maneuver
D: usually occurs bilaterally
column2:
Answer: A. usually occurs on left side

Feedback:
Varicoceles usually form on the left side of the scrotal sac due to the course of the left gonadal vein through the abdomen and
the increased risk of compression.
Note vessel 11 (left renal vein) travels between the SMA and aorta. Vessel 26 (left gonadal vein) is a tributary of the LRV. If the
LRV is compressed as it courses between the SMA and aorta, the LRV can dilate and the left gonadal vein will become engorged.
This will then lead to dilatation of the venous drainage at the left testicle causing a varicocele formation.

column1:
The image demonstrates a complication that is most common with what type of carcinoma?

A: Hepatic
B: Renal
C: Splenic
D: Pancreatic
column2:
Answer: B. Renal

Feedback:
Renal carcinoma has been shown to infiltrate the renal vein and IVC causing mass and thrombus formation within. It is the most
common type of tumor with IVC/renal vein invasion.

column1:
Which of the following is least likely to be associated with renal artery stenosis?

A: Scleroderma
B: Fibromuscular dysplasia
C: CREST syndrome
D: May Thurner syndrome
column2:
Answer: D. May Thurner syndrome

Feedback:
May Thurner syndrome involves the compression of the left common iliac vein against the lumbar vertebrae by the overlying
right common iliac artery which leads to thrombosis.
Scleroderma and CREST syndrome involve systemic sclerosis of multiple vessels.
Fibromuscular dysplasia is also associated with stenosis of the renal arteries.

column1:
Median arcuate ligament syndrome involves compression of which vessel during respiration?

A: superior mesenteric artery
B: main portal vein
C: inferior mesenteric artery
D: celiac axis
column2:
Answer: D. celiac axis

Feedback:
The median arcuate ligament connects the right and left crura of the diaphragm across the anterior proximal abdominal aorta.
The celiac axis origin is very close to the diaphragm and in some patients the median arcuate ligament crosses anterior to the
celiac origin. Median arcuate ligament syndrome is caused by the ligament compressing the celiac axis with respiration. The
aorta and branches move superiorly upon expiration. If the median arcuate ligament is compressing the celiac axis, symptoms
will be emphasized upon expiration.

column1: When measuring the AP diameter of the true lumen of the aneurysm, where should the caliper be placed at the anterior edge? Use your mouse to position the cursor at the correct caliper location and click to set your cursor.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The AP measurement of the aorta is obtained where the US beam is most perpendicular to the vessel wall. The aorta should be
measured from outer wall to outer wall. When thrombus accumulates in an aneurysm, the true lumen diameter should also be
reported. The internal dimensions of the lumen are recorded and Doppler evaluation should be performed to document flow
through the lumen.

column1:
Flow distal to a significant stenosis will exhibit which of the following characteristics?

A: higher resistance flow
B: lower resistance flow
C: increased velocity
D: increased resistive index
column2:
Answer: B. lower resistance flow

Feedback:
Flow distal to a significant stenosis will demonstrate reduced velocity and resistance.

column1:
Which of the following is a potential cause for the Doppler findings?

A: congestive heart failure or severe tricuspid regurgitation
B: aortic coarctation or abdominal aortic aneurysm
C: mitral regurgitation or tachycardia
D: mitral stenosis or premature ventricular contractions
column2:
Answer: A. congestive heart failure or severe tricuspid regurgitation

Feedback:
Anything that causes IVC congestion and dilatation will affect flow in the hepatic veins. CHF, cardiomyopathy, severe tricuspid
regurgitation or stenosis, and hepatic congestion can cause these changes to the hepatic and portal venous flow patterns. If
hepatic vein flow into the IVC is reduced due to IVC dilatation, the amount and velocity of retrograde systolic flow will increase.
Note the majority of the flow is retrograde on the hepatic vein tracing. If hepatic congestion occurs, the portal flow will be
affected. Flow in the portal vein becomes biphasic with hepatic congestion. The other answer choices either have no effect or
little effect on the right heart circulation/venous flow pattern.

column1:
The resistive index of the renal parenchymal arteries in cases of main renal artery stenosis will _________________ and the resistive index of the renal parenchymal arteries in cases of renal parenchymal disease will _________________

A: increase, remain normal
B: increase, decrease
C: remain normal, decrease
D: decrease, increase
column2:
Answer: D. decrease, increase

Feedback:
The resistive index of the renal parenchymal arteries in cases of main renal artery stenosis will decrease to below 0.5. The
resistance distal to a significant stenosis decreases and a tardus parvus waveform is possible. The resistive index of the renal
parenchymal arteries in cases of renal parenchymal disease will increase. As the ability of the kidney to function decreases, it
accepts less blood volume for filtration. The parenchymal resistance to flow increases and the resistive index value rises above
0.7.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the image?

A: The Doppler tracing obtained from the segmental artery will provide inaccurate velocity measurements.
B: The Doppler tracing obtained from the interlobar artery will provide inaccurate velocity measurements.
C: The Doppler tracing of the interlobar artery demonstrates normal flow and accurate velocity measurements can be obtained.
D: The Doppler tracing of the segmental artery demonstrates normal flow and accurate velocity measurements can be obtained.
column2:
Answer: A. The Doppler tracing obtained from the segmental artery will provide inaccurate velocity measurements.

Feedback:
The Doppler cursor is placed in the segmental artery of the kidney. Because no angle correction was used, the velocity
information will produce inaccurate information on flow velocity.

column1:
All of the following terms refer to the areas of flow separation seen distal to a stenosis, except:

A: autoregulation
B: eddy currents
C: flow vortices
D: post stenotic turbulence
column2:
Answer: A. autoregulation

Feedback:
Eddy currents are also known as flow vortices. These areas of flow separation are seen with post-stenotic turbulence.

column1:
This Doppler tracing taken from a branch of the aorta demonstrates:

A: Normal celiac flow with top normal PSV
B: Hemodynamically significant stenosis of the right renal artery
C: Hemodynamically significant stenosis of the left renal artery
D: Critical stenosis of the celiac axis
column2:
Answer: D. Critical stenosis of the celiac axis

Feedback:
Note the spectral broadening, increased PSV over 5 m/s (<2.0 NL) and increased diastolic flow. These Doppler characteristics
should lead to a diagnosis of hemodynamically significant stenosis of the celiac artery. The renal arteries would not normally be
evaluated with Doppler in the longitudinal view of the aorta. They also would not originate from the anterior aorta.

column1:
The renal-aortic ratio is invalid in patients with:

A: diabetes
B: medullary sponge kidney
C: abdominal aortic aneurysm
D: renal vein thrombosis
column2:
Answer: C. abdominal aortic aneurysm

Feedback:
The flow velocity in the aorta will be decreased with the presence of a AAA. A lower aortic velocity will abnormally elevate the
RAR.

column1:
The images demonstrated are from a mesenteric Doppler exam. The patient was NPO for the exam. The IMA was also evaluated and demonstrated a monophasic, high resistance waveform with a peak velocity of 150cm/s. What do these findings indicate?

A: Normal exam
B: Normal SMA and IMA flow but a tardus parvus waveform is identified in the celiac axis.
C: SMA stenosis
D: Mesenteric ischemia
column2:
Answer: C. SMA stenosis

Feedback:
Mesenteric ischemia requires stenosis or occlusion of at least two of the mesenteric arteries. (Celiac, SMA, or IMA) Only SMA
stenosis is present. The other velocities are normal for the IMA and celiac.

column1:
The majority of abdominal aortic aneurysms form in what part of the vessel?

A: Arch
B: Juxtarenal
C: Suprarenal
D: Infrarenal
column2:
Answer: D. Infrarenal

Feedback:
Pararenal, juxtarenal and suprarenal AAA can cause serious complications related to blood flow to the kidneys and renal
induced HTN. These types of AAA are considered a critical finding at any size. Infra renal AAA formation is the most common
AAA location. Fortunately this type of AAA can be monitored over time until it reaches 5.5-6cm in size when surgical intervention
is necessary.

column1:
Anemia will _______________ blood flow velocities detected on Doppler evaluation.

A: decrease
B: have no effect on venous
C: have no effect on arterial
D: increase
column2:
Answer: D. increase

Feedback:
Anemia indicates thinner blood with reduced viscosity that would move more quickly in all blood vessels.

column1:
Which type of aortic aneurysm is associated with an infection of the arterial wall?

A: Ectatic
B: Mycotic
C: Saccular
D: Berry
column2:
Answer: B. Mycotic

Feedback:
A mycotic aneurysm is caused by infection of the arterial wall which weakens it and allows aneurysm formation.

column1:
A damped waveform with low velocity turbulent systolic flow will be found:

A: in a normal PTA tracing
B: proximal to a significant stenosis
C: distal to a significant stenosis
D: at the stenosis site
column2:
Answer: C. distal to a significant stenosis

Feedback:
Distal to the site of stenosis, the blood flow will be lower velocity than at the stenosis. Turbulence will also cause spectral
broadening.

column1:
Hemodynamically significant stenosis of the SMA is diagnosed when the peak systolic velocity is greater than?

A: 1.0 m/s
B: 1.75 m/s
C: 2.0 m/s
D: 2.75 m/s
column2:
Answer: D. 2.75 m/s

Feedback:
Hemodynamically significant stenosis of the SMA is diagnosed with the PSV greater than 2.75 m/s.

column1:
Which of the following indicates a significant SMA stenosis?

A: pain in the abdomen when hungry that is relieved by eating/digestion
B: dilated inferior mesenteric artery that is easily visualized sonographically
C: PSV >200cm/s
D: mesenteric - aortic ratio > 2.0
column2:
Answer: B. dilated inferior mesenteric artery that is easily visualized sonographically

Feedback:
The IMA is usually very tough to find in most patients with ultrasound. If the artery is visible and easily evaluated, it usually
means it is dilated due to compensatory flow related to an SMA stenosis. Increased flow is being passed to the IMA due to the
obstruction in the SMA. A PSV greater than 2.75cm/s indicates significant stenosis. If the mesenteric - aortic ratio exceeds 3.0, a
significant stenosis is present. SMA disease causes pain after eating due to inadequate blood supply caused by the stenosis.

column1:
Why is aspirin prescribed for patients with atherosclerotic arterial disease?

A: to increase platelet production
B: to increase blood volume in the venous system
C: to reduce blood viscosity
D: to increase hemoglobin levels
column2:
Answer: C. to reduce blood viscosity

Feedback:
Aspirin reduces the risk of thrombus formation by inhibiting platelet aggregation. It thins the blood and allows it to move more
freely through a narrowed vessel.

column1:
When scanning a patient with a history of renal cell carcinoma, you identify a hypoechoic mass within the IVC lumen near the renal veins. What is the best way to determine if this is tumor invasion or thrombus formation?

A: Apply color Doppler to assess internal vascularity of the mass.
B: Evaluate the iliac veins for thrombosis.
C: Evaluate the aorta for tumor invasion.
D: Roll the patient into the decubitus position to evaluate the possible mobility of the mass.
column2:
Answer: A. Apply color Doppler to assess internal vascularity of the mass.

Feedback:
The mass should be evaluated for internal vascularity that would indicate tumor invasion. If there is not internal vascularity, the
obstruction is most likely a thrombus. The iliac veins should be evaluated if the mass causes IVC obstruction, but the question
refers to mass characterization.

column1:
Which of the following statements correctly describes the image of the aorta?

A: The AAA shown requires immediate treatment due to its location proximal to the renal arteries.
B: The total AP dimension of the AAA size is the distance between the actual vessel walls.
C: Embolism formation has occurred in the area of dilatation due to swirling and stasis of blood.
D: The true lumen is the distance between the actual vessel walls.
column2:
Answer: B. The total AP dimension of the AAA size is the distance between the actual vessel walls.

Feedback:
It is important to measure the AAA size and the actual lumen size, if thrombus accumulation is present within the AAA. The true
lumen is the anechoic space in the middle of the vessel. The total AP dimension of the AAA size is the distance between the
actual vessel walls. The AAA demonstrates thrombus accumulation along the walls. An embolism is a moving thrombus that gets
trapped in a distal smaller vessel. Embolisms are very rarely seen travelling in a vessel.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the symptoms associated with median arcuate ligament syndrome?

A: abdominal pain that is relieved by deep inspiration
B: abdominal pain that is relieved by eating a fatty meal
C: calf pain relieved by stretching the calf muscles
D: groin pain that is relieved by placing the legs in the dependent position
column2:
Answer: A. abdominal pain that is relieved by deep inspiration

Feedback:
The median arcuate ligament compresses the celiac axis on expiration. When the diaphragm moves cephalad, the artery is
pinched and abdominal symptoms occur. When the patient inhales, the diaphragm moves caudal and symptoms are relieved.

column1:
Which of the following sonographic characteristics is most suggestive of renal vein thrombosis?

A: reduced diastolic flow in the renal arteries
B: prominent flow in the segmental and intralobar arteries
C: turbulent venous flow within the kidney
D: low resistance flow in the intrarenal arteries
column2:
Answer: A. reduced diastolic flow in the renal arteries

Feedback:
Renal vein thrombosis will demonstrate increased kidney size, hypoechoic renal cortex, decreased corticomedullary
differentiation, mottled echogenicity, dilated renal vein and no intrarenal venous flow, and increased resistance in renal
arteries. Diastolic flow is reduced in the segmental and intralobular arteries due to increased parenchymal resistance. The
parenchymal RI will be >0.7.

column1:
Which of the following is an autosomal dominant disorder that can lead to arteriovenous malformation in the liver?

A: Klippel–Trenaunay syndrome
B: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia
C: May Thurner syndrome
D: Paget Schroetter syndrome
column2:
Answer: B. Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia

Feedback:
Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia, AKA Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome, is an autosomal dominant disorder that affects the
vasculature of multiple organs. It is characterized by multiple arteriovenous malformations (AVMs). Most commonly the AVMs
are superficially located in the skin, but can also occur in pulmonary, cerebral, GI tract, and liver vasculature. Contrast
echocardiography can be used to diagnose a suspected pulmonary AVM. Ultrasound is most helpful in evaluating liver AVMs.
The malformations can be arteriovenous or portovenous. Ultrasound evaluation can demonstrate signs of portal HTN due to
increased portal flow and increased resistance in the hepatic arterial flow with abnormal arterial connections. The disorder can
cause mesenteric angina from the steal phenomenon .

column1:
What effect will the findings on the image have on the Doppler tracings obtained in the iliac veins?

A: Both iliac veins will demonstrate continuous flow
B: Both iliac veins will demonstrate pulsatile flow
C: Both iliac veins will demonstrate phasic flow
D: Both iliac veins will demonstrate normal flow patterns due to the extensive venous network in the abdomen that allows for multiple collateral pathways
column2:
Answer: A. Both iliac veins will demonstrate continuous flow

Feedback:
Renal carcinoma has been shown to infiltrate the renal vein and IVC causing mass and thrombus formation within. It is the most
common type of tumor with IVC/renal vein invasion. Obstruction of the IVC will cause flow in the iliacs to become continuous.
Continuous flow in an extremity vein typically indicates a proximal obstruction is present.

column1:
Congestive heart failure causes increased pulsatility in the:

A: renal arteries and veins
B: bilateral superficial femoral arteries
C: bilateral carotid arteries
D: iliac veins and portal veins
column2:
Answer: D. iliac veins and portal veins

Feedback:
CHF causes reduced cardiac output and a backlog if flow in the venous system. The systemic and portal venous system can
demonstrate increased pulsatility with CHF. The decreased cardiac output can reduce pulsatility in the distal arterial system.

column1:
An aortic dissection most commonly originates:

A: in the descending thoracic aorta, just distal to the origin of the left subclavian artery
B: in the infrarenal descending aorta
C: in the aortic isthmus
D: in the ascending aorta
column2:
Answer: D. in the ascending aorta

Feedback:
Aortic dissection most commonly occurs in the ascending aorta. The descending thoracic aorta, just distal to the origin of the
left subclavian artery is the second most common site for dissection.

column1:
Budd Chiari syndrome refers to acute or chronic obstruction of which vessel(s)?

A: Splenic veins
B: Hepatic artery
C: Hepatic veins
D: Splenic artery
column2:
Answer: C. Hepatic veins

Feedback:
Obstruction of hepatic veins by thrombus or tumor is referred to as Budd Chiari syndrome. It can lead to symptoms and
complications such as hepatic congestion, hepatomegaly, ascites, RUQ pain and jaundice. The image below demonstrates
thrombus within the hepatic veins and no flow is detected by color Doppler.

column1:
Blue Toe Syndrome is a complication typically seen with which of the following?

A: Abdominal aortic aneurysm
B: Deep venous thrombosis
C: Thoracic outlet syndrome
D: Raynaud Phenomenon
column2:
Answer: A. Abdominal aortic aneurysm

Feedback:
Blue toe syndrome is caused by emboli reaching the small digital arteries and causing obstruction/ischemia. The ischemic toe
tissue becomes discolored and cyanotic in appearance. AAA formation usually leads to thrombus accumulation in the affected
area of the vessel. This thrombus poses a risk of emboli formation and distal ischemia.

column1:
The Bernoulli principle explains the:

A: increase in pressure seen with increased blood flow velocity
B: decrease in pressure seen with increased blood flow velocity
C: decrease in resistance seen with increased blood flow velocity
D: decrease in pressure seen with decreased blood flow velocity
column2:
Answer: B. decrease in pressure seen with increased blood flow velocity

Feedback:
The Bernoulli principle explains the decrease in pressure seen with increased blood flow velocity.

column1:
Which of the following is a Doppler indication of acute renal vein thrombosis?

A: resistive index >0.7 in the cortex
B: enlarged kidney
C: increased diastolic flow D d d ti l i t
D: decreased cortical resistance
column2:
Answer: A. resistive index >0.7 in the cortex

Feedback:
Acute renal vein thrombosis will lead to increased resistance in the arcuate arteries with an RI value >0.7 due to the obstructed
outflow. The kidney will enlarge but this is a 2D finding.

column1:
Which of the following is true regarding the Doppler tracing of the SMA?

A: normal SMA flow pattern pre-prandially
B: normal SMA flow pattern post-prandially
C: SMA stenosis
D: There is a pseudoaneurysm of the SMA causing very turbulent flow.
column2:
Answer: C. SMA stenosis

Feedback:
The peak velocity of the flow is over 2.75m/s and there is a high level of diastolic flow which indicates a stenosis greater than
75%.

column1:
What term refers to the phenomenon of a PW Doppler tracing displaying a wider distribution of the velocity of blood cells with varying degrees of arterial blockage?

A: Turbulence
B: Occlusion
C: Laminar flow
D: Spectral broadening
column2:
Answer: D. Spectral broadening

Feedback:
Spectral broadening refers to PW Doppler tracing displaying a wider distribution of the velocity of blood cells with varying
degrees of arterial blockage. Laminar flow refers to the slight difference in speed in the layers of blood within a vessel. An
occlusion would not demonstrate any blood velocity on a PW Doppler tracing. Turbulence refers to the disruption of laminar
flow causing eddy currents within the vessel. Turbulence is displayed as spectral broadening on a PW Doppler tracing.

column1:
According to the Bernoulli Principle, where will you find the lowest pressure in a vessel with stenosis?

A: distal to the stenosis
B: in the collateral vessels
C: at the site of stenosis
D: proximal to the stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. at the site of stenosis

Feedback:
Pressure is lowest at the stenotic site which causes an increased pressure gradient from proximal to the stenosis to the stenosis
site. This increased gradient leads to increased velocity of flow across the stenotic site.

column1:
An aortic aneurysm is diagnosed when the diameter of the aorta exceeds ___________ and a popliteal aneurysm is diagnosed when the diameter of the popliteal artery exceeds _______.

A: 3.5cm, 1cm
B: 3cm, 1cm
C: 3cm, 3cm
D: 3.5cm, 2cm
column2:
Answer: B. 3cm, 1cm

Feedback:
An aortic aneurysm is diagnosed when the diameter of the aorta exceeds 3cm and a popliteal aneurysm is diagnosed when the
diameter of the popliteal artery exceeds 1cm.

column1:
The Doppler tracing of the flow in a vessel just distal to a stenosis will demonstrate _____________ than at the stenosis.

A: more laminar flow
B: greater bandwidth
C: greater resistance
D: greater Doppler shift
column2:
Answer: B. greater bandwidth

Feedback:
Turbulence will cause a large range of different velocities (frequency shifts). The increased bandwidth is displayed as spectral
broadening and irregular waveform contour(post stenotic turbulence).

column1:
According to the Bernoulli principle, the velocity increases at an area of stenosis. What happens to the pressure at the site of stenosis?

A: increases
B: it increases exponentially
C: decreases
D: remains constant
column2:
Answer: C. decreases

Feedback:
The pressure proximal to the stenosis is very high. The pressure at the stenosis is very low causing an increased pressure
gradient between the two sites. The higher the pressure gradient, the higher the velocity.

column1:
Which of the following is a congenital vascular abnormality that commonly causes decreased ankle pressures bilaterally?

A: Coarctation of the aorta
B: Budd Chiari Syndrome
C: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome
D: Congestive heart failure
column2:
Answer: A. Coarctation of the aorta

Feedback:
Coarctation causes a ridge of tissue that blocks flow, usually in the descending aorta just distal to the origin of the left
subclavian artery. This causes lower resistance, monophasic flow with increased antegrade diastolic flow in the aortic segments
distal to the obstruction. Brachial pressures will be normal, but the bilateral ankle pressures will be decreased due to the lower
resistance flow and reduced flow volume reaching the ankles.

column1:
What is the parenchymal resistance ratio for the tracing displayed?

A: 30
B: 0.17
C: 6
D: 0.06
column2:
Answer: B. 0.17

Feedback:
Parenchymal Resistance Ratio or End Diastolic Ratio is used to assess parenchymal resistance in renal transplant cases. PRR
<0.2 indicates increased resistance and possible rejection. PRR = EDV / PSV = 6 / 36 = 0.17

column1:
Longitudinal images are presented of the anterior abdomen just superior to the umbilicus. What is the most common cause of the abnormality seen on the images?

A: portal HTN
B: polycythemia vera
C: malignant HTN
D: pulmonary HTN
column2:
Answer: A. portal HTN

Feedback:
A patent umbilical vein is identified on the images. Severe portal HTN will cause the umbilical vein to recanalize, allowing
retrograde filling to occur within the vessel.

column1:
You are performing a renal artery Doppler exam for renal stenosis. The mid aortic peak velocity is 70cm/s. The RRA peak velocity is 140cm/s and the LRA peak velocity is 240cm/s. What is the RAR for the right kidney?

A: 2
B: 0.5
C: 3.4
D: 0.3
column2:
Answer: A. 2

Feedback:
RRA 140cm/s / aorta 70cm/s = 2; LRA 240cm/s / aorta 70cm/s = 3.4

column1: Where should the second caliper be placed to measure the acceleration time of the renal artery waveform? Use your mouse to position the cursor and click to set your answer.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
Normal renal artery waveforms demonstrate an early systolic peak. When measuring the AT, the first caliper is placed at the
start of systole and the second caliper is placed at the top of the early systolic peak. When measuring the resistive index, the
first caliper is placed at the true peak systolic velocity and the second caliper is placed at the peak end diastolic velocity.

column1:
The most common site for renal artery stenosis is:

A: in the segmental artery of the upper pole
B: in the segmental artery of the lower pole
C: at the origin of the renal artery
D: at the renal hilum
column2:
Answer: C. at the origin of the renal artery

Feedback:
The most common site for renal artery stenosis is at the renal artery origin.

column1: When measuring the AP diameter of the aortic aneurysm, where should the caliper be placed at the anterior wall? Use your mouse to position the cursor at the correct caliper location and click to set your cursor.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The AP measurement of the aorta is obtained where the US beam is most perpendicular to the vessel wall. The aorta should be
measured from outer wall to outer wall.

column1:
Which of the following terms would be used to describe flow within an abdominal aortic aneurysm?

A: plug flow
B: non-laminar flow
C: triphasic flow
D: high resistance flow
column2:
Answer: B. non-laminar flow

Feedback:
Flow within the AAA will be decreased in resistance and turbulent due to the swirling effect in the enlarged segment. Normal
aortic flow is called plug flow because the majority of the blood moves at the same velocity. Flow in a AAA would be of many
different velocities and is considered non-laminar.

column1:
Which of the following vessels will be abnormally dilated with significant portal HTN?

A: main portal vein and hepatic veins
B: hepatic veins only
C: left gastric vein and hepatic veins
D: main portal vein and left gastric vein
column2:
Answer: D. main portal vein and left gastric vein

Feedback:
The left gastric vein (AKA coronary vein) dilates with significant portal HTN It normally drains into the splenic vein near the portal
confluence and is best evaluated in sagittal plane near porta hepatis. A dilated, patent umbilical vein is also a sign of significant
portal HTN. It is best evaluated in the sagittal plane to the left of midline at the level of the ligamentum teres. It acts as a
reservoir for portal blood with nowhere to go.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding the Doppler evaluation of the liver vasculature displayed?

A: The hepatic artery and portal vein are demonstrated with normal flow directions.
B: The hepatic artery is hepatopetal and the portal vein is hepatofugal.
C: The hepatic artery is hepatofugal and the portal vein is hepatopetal.
D: The portal vein flow is normal but the hepatic artery demonstrates increased resistance
column2:
Answer: B. The hepatic artery is hepatopetal and the portal vein is hepatofugal.

Feedback:
Hepatopetal = flow toward the liver; Hepatofugal = flow away from the liver; The hepatic artery and portal vein are both
normally hepatopetal. The image displays ABNORMAL flow direction in the portal vein caused by portal HTN.

column1:
A patient complains of bilateral pain and color changes in the legs. When he lies down, both legs become pale. When he sits up on the edge of the bed, both legs become reddened. These clinical findings are most suggestive of:

A: significant venous obstruction
B: deep venous reflux
C: normal arterial flow
D: significant aortic stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. significant aortic stenosis

Feedback:
Pallor and dependent rubor are indications of significant arterial disease. The bilateral presentation indicates the obstruction is
blocking flow to both legs = aorta.

column1:
The diagnosis of AAA is made once the aorta AP or width dimension exceeds _____.

A: 2.5cm
B: 3.0cm
C: 3.5cm
D: 4.0cm
column2:
Answer: B. 3.0cm

Feedback:
The diagnosis of AAA is made once the aorta AP or width dimension exceeds 3.0cm.

column1: Using the 3rd waveform, place the first cursor used to begin the resistive index measurement. Use your mouse to position the cursor and click to set your answer.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
Normal renal artery waveforms demonstrate an early systolic peak. When measuring the AT, the first caliper is placed at the
start of systole and the second caliper is placed at the top of the early systolic peak. When measuring the resistive index, the
first caliper is placed at the true peak systolic velocity and the second caliper is placed at the peak end diastolic velocity.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding the aorta?

A: Suprarenal AAAs normally do not affect the arterial flow to the kidneys.
B: The most common location for an aortic dissection is at the iliac bifurcation.
C: The majority of the aortic branches that lead to abdominal organs are lateral branches.
D: Rupture is the most common complication of an aortic aneurysm.
column2:
Answer: D. Rupture is the most common complication of an aortic aneurysm.

Feedback:
Rupture is the most common complication of an aortic aneurysm.
The most common location for an aortic dissection is in the ascending aorta. The majority of the aortic branches that lead to
abdominal organs are anterior branches. A suprarenal AAA would slow the flow to the kidneys and can affect their function.
Infrarenal AAAs normally do not affect the arterial flow to the kidneys.

column1:
The images displayed are from a renal Doppler exam on a 15yr old female with significant HTN. What is the most likely cause for the abnormality demonstrated?

A: Suprarenal AAA
B: Atherosclerotic disease
C: Infrarenal AAA
D: Fibromuscular dysplasia
column2:
Answer: D. Fibromuscular dysplasia

Feedback:
The bilateral presentation of renal stenosis in a pediatric patient is most likely due to a diffuse disorder of congenital or
idiopathic nature. Atherosclerosis formation is a chronic disease. Suprarenal AAA would reduce the blood flow to the kidneys
increasing the BP but would not cause the increase in velocity in the arteries.

column1:
All of the following terms could be used to describe the attached waveform, except:

A: delayed acceleration
B: reduced deceleration time
C: low resistance
D: tardus parvus
column2:
Answer: B. reduced deceleration time

Feedback:
The waveform demonstrates a slow upstroke (long AT) and late systolic peak that is consistent with a monophasic, tardus
parvus waveform. The deceleration is delayed not shortened.

column1:
Increased resistance in a renal artery can indicate:

A: proximal stenosis or kidney disease
B: normal post-prandial flow
C: normal pre-prandial flow
D: distal stenosis or kidney disease
column2:
Answer: D. distal stenosis or kidney disease

Feedback:
Increased resistance in a renal artery can indicate distal stenosis or kidney disease. If the renal artery demonstrates increased
resistance to flow, there is either a vascular blockage distally or the kidney parenchyma is resisting the arterial inflow because it
cannot properly filter the blood. Any kidney disease that affects the filtration system will lead to increased parenchymal
resistance to arterial inflow. Normal renal artery flow is low resistance with antegrade diastolic flow.

column1:
Which of the following is associated with spectral broadening?

A: laminar flow
B: increased bandwidth
C: pulsatile flow
D: blunted waveform
column2:
Answer: B. increased bandwidth

Feedback:
Bandwidth refers to the difference between the highest and lowest frequencies in the pulse. Laminar flow has a narrow
bandwidth of reflected signals because the blood cells are all moving at relatively the same speed. This allows for the display of
a spectral window on the tracing. With turbulent flow, the blood cells are moving at many different velocities. This produces a
wide bandwidth of reflected signals and the spectral window fills in.
